title: International Law
rationale: The definitive single-volume textbook reviewing all major domains of public international law. Shaw's 8th edition (2017, CUP, ISBN 9781107188471, 1118pp) is universally adopted in top law schools (Cambridge, Oxford, NYU, LSE) and cited in ICJ pleadings. Hardback ISBN verified via Google Books (1118pp), Amazon (1118pp), Wildy's Legal Bookshop, and Cambridge Core. Selected as the root foundational textbook for the international-law leaf.
excerpt: The definitive single-volume textbook on public international law, offering a comprehensive, authoritative, and critically engaged survey of the field from sources and subjects to dispute settlement and use of force.
keyIdeas: International law is a complete legal system, not merely a set of moral obligations between states
Treaties and custom are the two primary sources; general principles fill gaps
Statehood requires population, territory, government, and independence — recognition is declaratory, not constitutive
The prohibition on the use of force is a cornerstone of the UN Charter, subject to narrow self-defence exceptions
State responsibility arises from breaches of international obligations; it is a secondary rule governing reparation
keyTakeaways: States remain the primary subjects of international law, but international organisations and individuals increasingly possess legal personality
Customary international law binds all states regardless of treaty ratification
Jus cogens norms (e.g., prohibition of genocide, torture) are peremptory and non-derogable
The ICJ is the principal judicial organ, but its jurisdiction depends on state consent
International humanitarian law and international criminal law have significantly expanded the individual accountability regime
whoShouldRead: Law students and legal practitioners specialising in public international law
Diplomats, foreign ministry officers, and policy advisers in international organisations
Scholars and researchers across international relations, political science, and global governance
Judges and arbitrators dealing with cross-border disputes
whoShouldSkip: Readers seeking a narrow casebook focused solely on litigation
Those interested exclusively in comparative domestic law without an international dimension
Practitioners needing specialist depth on niche subfields like space or investment treaty arbitration
